title: D100 Dungeon (with World Builder) - The Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WIP6ZDjQmww

Closing the series with the verdict. Daniel (@dungeondive) reviews the package *D100 Dungeon core book + World Builder expansion (+ optional mapping game)* as a single experience and lands on *easy top 20, possibly top 10*. The recommended entry point is the two books — around £80 with the mapping box, considerably less in PDF — and the case for the bundle rests on five upsides: an almost-endless sense of adventure built from your own hand-drawn world; a fast learn-and-relearn curve; *connect-the-dots* emergent narrative (better, Daniel argues, than scripted board-game stories); low table-setup overhead; and a current of *exciting because slow* decision-making, particularly around press-your-luck searches and event-table chains.

The four downsides are equally specific. Weapon variety is thin — sword-and-board or two-hander, then optimise damage — and he floats a house rule grouping weapons by class with situational bonuses. Quest text is *bare bones*, requiring the player to do the colour work. Unlucky rolls can wedge a quest, so Daniel uses a self-applied improving-probability fix on long item hunts. Rules are scattered across multiple expansion books — same issue as *Four Against Darkness*. And the bookkeeping is heavy, though Daniel reframes the ledger as *an adventure diary* worth keeping. He exits the series wanting to keep the table set up indefinitely.

----

Daniel reframes heavy bookkeeping as *memorialising the time spent with the game*. Which solo systems have left you with a stack of paper you actually wanted to keep?
